name: CAPEC-586: Insecure Deserialization via Object Injection
description: Attacker controlling serialized object input can craft a malicious payload exploiting deserialization gadget chains in the application's classpath, leading to remote code execution or arbitrary object instantiation with application-level privileges.
mitigations: Validate and allowlist deserializable types before deserialization occurs
Use data formats that cannot carry code (JSON/XML with schemas) instead of native serialization
Implement integrity checks (HMAC) on serialized data to detect tampering before parsing
Keep deserialization libraries patched; audit classpath for known gadget chains with tools like ysoserial

name: CAPEC-135: Format String Injection
description: Attacker with control over format string arguments passed to functions like printf/sprintf can supply format specifiers (%x, %s, %n) to read arbitrary memory regions or write to arbitrary addresses, leaking sensitive data or corrupting process state.
mitigations: Never pass user-controlled data as the format string argument — always use a literal format string
Enable compiler format string warnings (-Wformat-security) and treat them as errors
Use safe string libraries that do not interpret format specifiers from user input
Fuzzing with format string payloads as part of security testing

name: CAPEC-136: LDAP Injection via Unvalidated Directory Query Input
description: Attacker supplying unvalidated input to an LDAP directory query can inject special characters (*, (, ), \, NUL) to manipulate filter logic, bypassing authentication checks or enumerating all directory entries.
mitigations: Encode all user-supplied values using LDAP distinguished name and filter encoding before query construction
Use parameterized LDAP APIs (RFC 4515 safe search filters) instead of string concatenation
Restrict the LDAP service account to read-only access on the minimum required subtree
Test LDAP endpoints with injection payloads: *, )(&, )(uid=*) as part of security assessment

name: CAPEC-101: Server-Side Include Injection
description: Attacker with control over content included in SSI-enabled web pages can inject SSI directives (<!--#exec cmd='...'-->) to execute arbitrary OS commands on the web server, access sensitive files, or modify page content.
mitigations: Disable SSI execution in directories that serve user-uploaded or user-controlled content
Sanitize user input to remove SSI directive syntax (<!--#)
Restrict SSI to specific, controlled directories using web server configuration
Run the web server process under a minimal-privilege user account

name: CAPEC-251: Local File Inclusion via Path Traversal
description: Attacker with control over file path parameters can traverse the directory structure using sequences like ../../ to read sensitive files outside the intended web root, including configuration files, SSH keys, and /etc/passwd.
mitigations: Resolve and validate that the canonical path starts with the expected base directory before opening
Use an allowlist of permitted filenames rather than accepting arbitrary user-supplied paths
Never pass user-controlled input directly to filesystem APIs
Run the application under a user with restricted filesystem access using chroot or containers

name: CAPEC-253: Remote File Inclusion
description: Attacker with control over file URL parameters in server-side include or require calls can supply a URL pointing to an attacker-controlled server, causing the application to download and execute malicious code with the application's privileges.
mitigations: Disable allow_url_include and allow_url_fopen in PHP configurations
Use an allowlist of permitted local file paths — never allow URLs as file references
Block outbound HTTP from the application server to prevent RFI payload retrieval
Enable AppArmor/SELinux profiles that restrict the application's file access scope

name: CAPEC-183: IMAP/SMTP Command Injection
description: Attacker with control over email address fields or mail headers can inject IMAP or SMTP protocol commands by embedding CRLF sequences, redirecting mail, exfiltrating mailbox contents, or using the mail server as a relay for further attacks.
mitigations: Sanitize email input by stripping or rejecting CR (\r) and LF (\n) characters before use in mail commands
Use well-maintained mail libraries that handle header injection prevention internally
Validate that email addresses conform to RFC 5321 syntax before passing to mail functions
Restrict the mail server to accept relay only from authorized application IP ranges

name: CAPEC-250: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection
description: Attacker with control over XML input to an application can define an external entity referencing a local file path or internal service URL, causing the XML parser to read and return the file contents or make SSRF requests on the application's behalf.
mitigations: Disable external entity processing in the XML parser (DOCTYPE declarations and entity resolution)
Use a modern XML library configured to reject DOCTYPE declarations entirely if XML schema does not require them
Validate and sanitize all XML input against a strict schema before parsing
Block outbound network access from the application server to prevent SSRF via XXE

name: CAPEC-134: Email Header Injection
description: Attacker with control over email form fields (To, From, Subject, CC) can inject CRLF characters to add arbitrary headers, redirecting emails, adding BCC recipients, or injecting spam content through the application's mail functionality.
mitigations: Strip CR and LF characters from all user-supplied values used in email headers
Use parameterized email APIs that separate headers from values at the library level
Validate To/From/CC fields against RFC 5321 patterns before constructing the message
Log all outbound email with recipient lists and alert on unexpectedly high send volumes

name: CAPEC-94: Adversary in the Middle (AiTM) Attack
description: Attacker with network positioning between client and server can intercept, read, and selectively modify encrypted or unencrypted traffic in real time, capturing credentials, session tokens, and sensitive data without either party detecting the interception.
mitigations: Enforce TLS with certificate pinning on all sensitive communications to prevent certificate substitution
Implement HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) with preloading to prevent SSL stripping
Use mutual TLS (mTLS) for service-to-service communication to authenticate both endpoints
Deploy network monitoring to detect ARP spoofing and rogue CA certificates

name: CAPEC-275: DNS Rebinding Attack
description: Attacker controlling a DNS domain can serve a short-TTL record that first resolves to a public IP (passing browser same-origin checks) then rebinds to an internal IP address, enabling malicious browser scripts to make requests to internal services on behalf of the victim.
mitigations: Validate the Host header in internal services and reject requests from unexpected hostnames
Configure DNS resolvers to block external domains from resolving to private IP ranges (DNS rebinding protection)
Implement token-based CSRF protection on all internal API endpoints
Bind internal services to loopback or specific internal interfaces rather than 0.0.0.0

name: CAPEC-90: Reflection Attack in Authentication Protocol
description: Attacker with access to a challenge-response authentication protocol can exploit symmetric handshake designs by reflecting the server's own challenge back at it, convincing the server it has verified a secret the attacker does not possess.
mitigations: Design authentication protocols so that challenges are directionally differentiated (server and client use distinct nonce spaces)
Use asymmetric cryptography (public/private keys) where the server never needs to prove knowledge of a shared secret
Include the session direction (initiator/responder) in the authenticated message to prevent reflection
Use established authentication protocols (TLS, SCRAM, SRP) rather than custom challenge-response designs

name: CAPEC-111: JSONP Callback Hijacking
description: Attacker with JavaScript injection capability on a page can override Array or Object constructors before a JSONP endpoint loads, intercepting JSON data intended only for the legitimate page and exfiltrating authenticated user data to an attacker-controlled server.
mitigations: Replace JSONP endpoints with CORS-enabled JSON APIs that validate the Origin header
If JSONP is unavoidable, validate the callback parameter against a strict alphanumeric allowlist
Prepend anti-hijacking prefixes (while(1);) or use Content-Type: application/json instead of text/javascript
Require authentication tokens in the JSONP request URL to prevent unauthenticated data exposure

name: CAPEC-219: XML Routing Detour Attack
description: Attacker with access to a SOAP or XML-routed messaging system can inject or modify WS-Addressing headers to reroute messages through an attacker-controlled intermediary node, enabling transparent interception and modification before forwarding to the destination.
mitigations: Digitally sign SOAP messages using WS-Security to detect header tampering in transit
Validate WS-Addressing routing headers against an allowlist of trusted intermediary nodes
Minimize the number of intermediary nodes permitted in the message routing path
Use mutual TLS between all nodes in the service mesh to prevent unauthorized intermediaries

name: CAPEC-384: API Message Manipulation via Adversary in the Middle
description: Attacker positioned between API client and server can intercept, modify, and replay API messages — changing request parameters, response data, or authentication tokens — to escalate privileges or trigger unintended server-side operations.
mitigations: Sign API request bodies with a shared secret (HMAC-SHA256) so the server can detect modifications
Use TLS with certificate pinning in API clients to prevent proxy interception
Implement request replay protection using nonce values or monotonic timestamps in request headers
Validate that response data has not been modified using response signing where critical

name: CAPEC-615: Evil Twin Wi-Fi Attack
description: Attacker broadcasting a rogue Wi-Fi access point with the same SSID as a trusted network can trick client devices into connecting automatically, capturing all plaintext traffic and harvesting credentials from users who do not verify TLS certificates.
mitigations: Enforce TLS with certificate pinning in all client applications to prevent credential capture on rogue networks
Configure device Wi-Fi policies to require 802.1X authentication for enterprise network connections
Educate users to verify HTTPS padlocks and not accept certificate warnings on sensitive sites
Deploy rogue access point detection in office environments using wireless intrusion detection

name: CAPEC-37: Retrieving Hardcoded Sensitive Data from Binaries
description: Attacker with access to distributed application binaries (APK, EXE, JAR, compiled Go) can use decompilation or string extraction tools to recover hardcoded credentials, API keys, encryption keys, or internal service URLs embedded by developers at compile time.
mitigations: Never hardcode secrets in source code — use environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime
Scan all build artifacts for secrets patterns using tools like truffleHog or gitleaks in CI
For mobile apps, use Android Keystore/iOS Keychain for credential storage — not strings in code
Rotate any credential found in a binary immediately and audit what access it granted

name: CAPEC-204: Mining Sensitive Data from Application Cache
description: Attacker with local system access can examine application cache directories (browser cache, HTTP response cache, ORM query cache) containing inadequately protected sensitive data, extracting credentials, PII, or session tokens persisted without expiry.
mitigations: Set Cache-Control: no-store on HTTP responses containing sensitive data
Encrypt sensitive values before writing to any disk-backed cache
Apply cache TTLs proportional to data sensitivity — shorter TTLs for credentials and session data
Clear caches on logout and on sensitive context changes (privilege escalation, account changes)

name: CAPEC-150: Data Harvesting from Common Resource Locations
description: Attacker with web or filesystem access can probe well-known paths (/.git/, /backup/, /.env, /config.yaml, /phpinfo.php, robots.txt excluded paths) to retrieve configuration files, database credentials, or application source code left accessible by misconfiguration.
mitigations: Block access to dotfiles (.env, .git, .htpasswd) and backup extensions (.bak, .old, .swp) at the web server level
Never deploy version control directories (.git/) into the web root
Scan deployments with tools like dirbuster or gobuster as part of security testing
Configure the web server to return 403 for directory listing and unknown file types

name: CAPEC-54: System Information Fingerprinting via Error Messages
description: Attacker probing the application with crafted requests can extract technology fingerprints (framework version, database type, server stack, internal paths) from verbose error pages, HTTP headers, and diagnostic endpoints, reducing the effort required to mount targeted attacks.
mitigations: Return generic error messages to clients — log detailed errors server-side only
Remove framework-specific headers (X-Powered-By, Server) from all HTTP responses
Disable diagnostic endpoints (phpinfo, /debug, Spring Actuator) in production
Implement custom error pages that do not expose stack traces or internal path information

name: CAPEC-116: Application Excavation via Differential Error Analysis
description: Attacker systematically sending valid but boundary-probing requests and observing differences in application responses (timing, error codes, response size) can map internal application structure, valid usernames, configuration parameters, and business logic without triggering obvious errors.
mitigations: Normalize responses to be constant-time and constant-size where feasible to prevent differential analysis
Return the same error structure regardless of whether the target resource exists (prevent enumeration)
Rate-limit and throttle requests from single sources to slow systematic probing
Implement anomaly detection to identify and block systematic enumeration patterns

name: CAPEC-621: Packet Timing Analysis to Infer Encrypted Content
description: Attacker with network access who cannot decrypt TLS traffic can analyze inter-packet timing, packet sizes, and traffic patterns to infer the type of activity (login, search, video streaming) or reconstruct sensitive information such as keystrokes or page content.
mitigations: Implement fixed-size packet padding to normalize traffic patterns for sensitive operations
Add randomized delays to responses for authentication and other sensitive endpoints
Use traffic obfuscation techniques (cover traffic) in high-security contexts
Prefer binary protocols with predictable payload sizes over text protocols that reveal content length

name: CAPEC-600: Credential Stuffing Attack
description: Attacker with a list of username/password pairs from previous data breaches can systematically try them against the application's login endpoint, successfully authenticating as users who reuse passwords across services, gaining access to accounts without knowing the actual passwords.
mitigations: Require multi-factor authentication — a second factor invalidates stuffed credentials even when the password matches
Implement adaptive rate limiting and CAPTCHA on login endpoints triggered by failure rate or velocity
Check submitted passwords against known-breached credential databases (HaveIBeenPwned API) at login
Detect and alert on distributed login attempts targeting many accounts with the same password

name: CAPEC-50: Password Recovery Mechanism Exploitation
description: Attacker targeting a user account can exploit weak password recovery flows — security questions with guessable answers, recovery tokens sent without rate limiting, or recovery links that do not expire — to gain account access without knowing the original password.
mitigations: Use email or SMS OTP as the recovery factor — avoid knowledge-based security questions
Expire recovery tokens after 15 minutes and invalidate them immediately after use
Rate-limit recovery requests by account and IP to prevent enumeration and brute force
Notify the account owner via an alternate channel when a recovery flow is initiated

name: CAPEC-59: Session Token Prediction via Insufficient Entropy
description: Attacker observing a sequence of session tokens generated with a weak or seeded pseudo-random number generator can predict future or past token values, forging valid session identifiers to hijack authenticated sessions without intercepting traffic.
mitigations: Generate session tokens using a cryptographically secure PRNG (os.urandom, secrets module) with at least 128 bits of entropy
Never seed the PRNG with predictable values (timestamps, PIDs, request counters)
Use established session management libraries rather than custom token generation
Rotate session tokens on privilege changes (login, role escalation) to limit prediction window

name: CAPEC-196: Session Token Forgery via Weak Signing
description: Attacker with knowledge of a weak or exposed signing secret can forge valid session tokens (JWT, signed cookies) with arbitrary claims, impersonating any user or elevating privileges without needing to steal an existing token.
mitigations: Use a strong, randomly generated secret of at least 256 bits for HMAC signing — never a guessable string
Store signing secrets in a secrets manager, not in environment variables or configuration files
Prefer asymmetric signing (RS256/ES256) for tokens so the private key never leaves the issuer
Implement key rotation and short token lifetimes to bound the blast radius of key compromise

name: CAPEC-114: Authentication State Machine Abuse
description: Attacker can exploit multi-step authentication workflows by manipulating the sequence of requests — skipping steps, replaying intermediate states, or sending requests out of order — to bypass portions of the authentication flow and gain access without completing all required checks.
mitigations: Track authentication state server-side using an opaque session token — never rely on client-supplied step indicators
Enforce strict sequential state transitions; any out-of-order step should invalidate the session
Test authentication flows with step-skipping and replay attacks as part of security assessment
Require re-authentication for sensitive operations rather than trusting long-lived session state

name: CAPEC-115: Authentication Bypass via Direct Resource Access
description: Attacker can access protected resources by directly requesting the resource URL, bypassing the authentication front-end or relying on a misconfiguration where the back-end resource lacks its own access control and relies solely on a gateway that is not enforced on all paths.
mitigations: Enforce authentication at the resource level, not only at the front controller or gateway
Use a centralized authorization middleware that applies to all routes by default, requiring explicit opt-out
Test all routes with unauthenticated requests as part of security scanning and CI
Map all application routes and verify each has a documented authentication requirement

name: CAPEC-87: Forceful Browsing to Access Unlinked Resources
description: Attacker aware of common resource naming conventions can directly request URLs for administrative pages, backup files, or user-specific resources by guessing or predicting paths, bypassing the UI-level access restriction that would normally prevent navigation to those resources.
mitigations: Authenticate and authorize every HTTP request at the server — never assume an unlinked resource is inaccessible
Use non-guessable identifiers (UUIDs) for user-specific resources rather than sequential IDs
Return 403 (not 404) for resources the authenticated user cannot access to avoid confirming existence
Automated security scanning of all application routes to verify access control enforcement

name: CAPEC-1: Accessing Functionality Not Constrained by ACLs (Broken Access Control)
description: Attacker with a low-privilege account can access administrative functions, other users' data, or restricted API endpoints that are listed in routes or discovered by enumeration but lack server-side authorization checks, relying instead on UI-level hiding.
mitigations: Implement a default-deny authorization model — every endpoint explicitly grants access, nothing is permitted by default
Perform access control checks in server-side code, never relying solely on client-side UI suppression
Write automated tests that assert every endpoint returns 401/403 when accessed without required permissions
Conduct periodic access control matrix audits mapping roles to permitted operations

name: CAPEC-77: Client-Side Variable and Parameter Tampering
description: Attacker modifying hidden form fields, URL parameters, cookies, or local storage values before submission can override price, quantity, role, or user_id values that the server trusts from the client, enabling price manipulation, business logic bypass, or unauthorized data access.
mitigations: Never trust client-supplied values for security-sensitive attributes — recompute them server-side from the authenticated session
Sign or MAC sensitive parameters that must round-trip through the client to detect tampering
Validate all submitted values against server-side business rules regardless of what the UI allows
Log parameter override attempts and alert on values that differ from expected ranges

name: CAPEC-39: Opaque Client Token Tampering
description: Attacker intercepting or modifying encrypted or obfuscated data tokens transmitted between client and server (e.g., price tokens, discount codes, capability grants) can alter the embedded values when the server decrypts them, exploiting weak integrity protection to change business-critical parameters.
mitigations: Use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM) rather than encryption alone — unauthenticated ciphertext can be modified without detection
Include an HMAC over all sensitive fields in the token to detect tampering before decryption
Prefer server-side state (session store) over client-side tokens for security-sensitive data
Validate decrypted token fields against server-side constraints before acting on them

name: CAPEC-202: Malicious Client-Side Code Modification
description: Attacker reverse-engineering a client application (mobile app, thick client, compiled JavaScript) can modify its logic — removing input validation, altering calculation results, or injecting additional API calls — and use the modified client to bypass controls the server assumes the client enforces.
mitigations: Never rely on client-side validation as the sole security control — duplicate all checks server-side
Implement server-side integrity verification of all inputs, especially security-relevant calculated values
Use code signing and runtime integrity checks (Android SafetyNet, iOS DeviceCheck) for mobile clients
Treat all client-submitted data as untrusted regardless of what the client code is supposed to do

name: CAPEC-207: Removing or Disabling Client-Side Security Controls
description: Attacker with access to a browser developer console or a proxy tool can disable or bypass client-side validation, content security policies, UI-level restrictions, or JavaScript-enforced rate limits, submitting requests the UI was designed to prevent.
mitigations: Implement all security-relevant validations server-side — consider client-side controls as UX only
Content Security Policy headers enforce script execution constraints but do not replace server validation
Perform integration tests that directly call API endpoints (bypassing the UI) to verify server-side enforcement
Monitor API usage patterns for requests that are structurally impossible through the normal UI

name: CAPEC-695: Dependency Repository Jacking
description: Attacker who discovers that a package name referenced in a project's dependency manifest is no longer maintained or has been deleted from the public registry can register a malicious package under that name, causing builds that install the package to execute attacker-controlled code with developer machine or CI/CD privileges.
mitigations: Pin all dependency versions to exact version numbers and verify checksums (lock files with integrity hashes)
Use a private artifact registry or repository proxy that only mirrors explicitly approved packages
Monitor for changes in ownership or publication of transitive dependencies
Regularly audit the dependency tree for packages with no active maintainers or unusual publication patterns

name: CAPEC-471: Library Search Order Hijacking (DLL Planting)
description: Attacker with write access to a directory earlier in an application's library search path can place a malicious shared library or DLL with the same name as a legitimate dependency, causing the application to load and execute the attacker's code with the application's privilege level when the library is imported.
mitigations: Use absolute paths when loading libraries — never rely on relative search path resolution
Verify library integrity using cryptographic signatures before loading
Set restrictive permissions on directories in the application's library search path
On Windows, enable Safe DLL Search Mode and use DELAYLOAD to control library loading order

name: CAPEC-132: Symlink Attack on Privileged File Operations
description: Attacker with write access to a directory where a privileged process creates temporary files can replace those files with symbolic links pointing to sensitive targets before the privileged process writes to them, causing the privileged process to overwrite arbitrary files on behalf of the attacker.
mitigations: Use O_NOFOLLOW flag when opening files to prevent following symlinks
Create temporary files in directories that only the owning process can write to (mkstemp in a private temp dir)
Check that the file opened is the file expected — verify inode after open to detect race conditions
Run privileged processes with minimal filesystem permissions using capabilities or namespaces

name: CAPEC-38: Configuration File Search Path Hijacking
description: Attacker with write access to directories earlier in an application's configuration search path can plant a malicious configuration file that overrides the legitimate configuration, redirecting the application to attacker-controlled endpoints, disabling security controls, or injecting malicious command parameters.
mitigations: Load configuration from a single, explicitly specified absolute path rather than searching multiple directories
Verify the integrity and provenance of configuration files using cryptographic signatures
Restrict write permissions on all directories in the configuration search path to privileged accounts only
Implement startup validation that confirms expected configuration values are present and within allowed ranges

name: CAPEC-630: Package Typosquatting Attack
description: Attacker registering a package name on npm, PyPI, or similar registries that closely resembles a popular legitimate package (one character transposition, added hyphen, common misspelling) can cause developers or CI/CD systems that mistype the package name to install and execute malicious code.
mitigations: Use package lock files with exact version pins and integrity hashes to prevent resolving to unexpected packages
Configure a private registry or dependency proxy that only allows pre-approved packages through
Add package name validation to code review — flag unfamiliar packages for verification
Register common typo variants of your own published package names to prevent squatting

name: CAPEC-632: Homograph Domain Attack via Visually Similar Characters
description: Attacker registering a domain name containing Unicode homoglyphs — characters that are visually identical or near-identical to ASCII characters (Cyrillic 'а' vs. Latin 'a') — can create convincing counterfeit sites or email domains that bypass visual inspection and some phishing filters.
mitigations: Enable browser internationalized domain name (IDN) restrictions that warn users about mixed-script domains
Use DMARC, DKIM, and SPF for your email domain and monitor for homograph variants sending mail
Register common homograph variants of your own domain to prevent impersonation
Deploy certificate transparency monitoring to detect fraudulent certificates issued for homograph domains

name: CAPEC-201: SSRF via Serialized Data External Entity Reference
description: Attacker with control over serialized data (XML, YAML, pickle, Java serialization) processed by the server can embed external references to internal URLs or file paths that the deserializer resolves on the server's behalf, enabling SSRF to cloud metadata endpoints or reading sensitive local files.
mitigations: Configure serialization libraries to reject external references (disable YAML anchors, XML external entities, pickle object imports)
Use data format parsers in safe mode that do not resolve URLs or file paths during parsing
Restrict outbound HTTP from the application server to prevent SSRF reachability of cloud metadata endpoints
Validate and sanitize serialized input against a strict schema before parsing

name: CAPEC-104: Cross-Zone Scripting via Trusted Zone Injection
description: Attacker who can inject content into a higher-trust browser zone (Local Machine Zone, intranet zone, or trusted site zone) can execute scripts with elevated privileges that bypass the restrictions applied to the Internet zone, reading local files or making privileged API calls.
mitigations: Apply Content Security Policy headers that restrict script sources regardless of zone
Validate and sanitize all content that may be rendered in any browser context, especially local HTML files
Configure browser enterprise policies to restrict Local Machine Zone script execution
Deliver application content over HTTPS to ensure it is always treated as Internet zone content

name: CAPEC-662: Adversary in the Browser via Malicious Extension
description: Attacker distributing a malicious browser extension through official extension stores can intercept and modify all HTTP traffic, capture form submissions, steal session cookies, and inject JavaScript into pages — bypassing HTTPS, HSTS, and multi-factor authentication by operating inside the trusted browser context.
mitigations: Restrict corporate devices to a pre-approved extension allowlist using browser enterprise policy
Educate users not to install extensions that request broad permissions (read all site data)
Monitor for new extensions installed on managed devices and alert on unapproved additions
Implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) for all CDN-loaded scripts to detect modification

name: CAPEC-595: TCP/IP Connection Reset via Forged RST Packets
description: Attacker with network access and knowledge of an active TCP connection's sequence numbers can send forged RST (reset) packets to abruptly terminate the connection, disrupting persistent connections such as database connections, long-polling APIs, SSH tunnels, or streaming media.
mitigations: Use TLS for all connections so that forged RST packets can be detected as invalid (connection state mismatch)
Implement application-level keepalive and reconnection logic to recover from unexpected connection drops
Deploy network monitoring to detect RST injection patterns from unexpected sources
Use TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) for infrastructure connections to authenticate TCP segments

name: CAPEC-240: Resource Injection via URL or Identifier Manipulation
description: Attacker with control over resource identifiers (file paths, URLs, database IDs, command arguments) passed to system functions can substitute unexpected resource references, causing the application to access, load, or operate on unintended resources outside the intended access scope.
mitigations: Validate all resource identifiers against an allowlist of permitted values or patterns before use
Use indirect reference maps that translate user-supplied keys to internal resource references server-side
Apply least-privilege to resource access — the application process should only be able to access what it legitimately needs
Log all resource access with the requesting identity to detect unauthorized resource probing

name: CAPEC-505: Mobile URL Scheme Squatting
description: Attacker publishing a mobile application that registers a URL scheme (e.g., myapp://) identical to a legitimate app's deep link scheme can intercept deep link invocations on devices where both apps are installed, capturing OAuth authorization codes, tokens, or sensitive parameters passed in the URL.
mitigations: Use Universal Links (iOS) and App Links (Android) verified via HTTPS instead of custom URL schemes for sensitive flows
Implement PKCE for OAuth flows so intercepted authorization codes are useless without the code verifier
Register App Links with SHA-256 certificate fingerprint verification to prevent scheme hijacking
Avoid passing sensitive tokens or credentials in URL parameters for deep links

name: CAPEC-616: Establishing Rogue Update Infrastructure
description: Attacker compromising an application's update distribution infrastructure or registering a domain similar to the update server can deliver malicious updates to clients that do not verify update signatures, replacing legitimate software with backdoored versions that execute with full user privileges.
mitigations: Sign all software updates with a private key and verify the signature on the client before installation
Use certificate pinning for the update server connection to prevent DNS hijacking redirecting clients to rogue servers
Deliver updates over TLS with HSTS to prevent downgrade attacks
Implement rollback protection so clients reject updates with version numbers lower than the current version

name: CAPEC-48: Local File Path Injection via URL Parameter
description: Attacker supplying a local file URI (file:///etc/passwd) as a URL parameter to a server-side function that fetches URLs without validating the scheme can cause the server to read arbitrary local files and return their contents in the response, bypassing authentication on the local filesystem.
mitigations: Validate the scheme and host of any user-supplied URL — allowlist only https:// to known external domains
Use a URL parsing library to extract and validate scheme, host, and path components before fetching
Block outbound requests to localhost, link-local addresses, and private IP ranges to prevent SSRF
Never pass user-supplied strings directly to HTTP client libraries without full URL validation
